comments:
This paper introduces a clear and well-motivated novel task — predicting whether an expressed desire by a discourse participant is fulfilled in the subsequent text — and provides two annotated datasets (MCTest-derived and SimpleWiki) together with a set of models and feature designs. The main technical contribution is a latent-structured narrative model (LSNM) that associates latent states with successive evidence fragments and predicts fulfillment from the sequence; the authors also propose a set of linguistically motivated features (focal words, subject mentions, connotation-based emotional/action cues, discourse markers) and compare the structured model against a textual-entailment baseline and unstructured classifiers.

Strengths:
- The task is well-motivated and has useful downstream applications (narrative understanding, forum moderation, opinion analysis).
- Providing two annotated datasets and releasing them will be valuable to the community.
- The LSNM is an appropriate and interpretable way to capture narrative flow and demonstrates consistent gains over baselines in the presented experiments.
- Feature design is linguistically sensible (focal words, connotation lexicon, discourse cues), and the paper situates itself well relative to prior narrative and RTE work.

Weaknesses and suggested improvements:
- Scope of desire expressions is limited: data collection was restricted to three surface patterns ("wanted to", "wished to", "hoped to"). This restricts coverage and may bias the datasets. Discussing how to extend to varied lexical/ syntactic realizations would strengthen the paper.
- The baselines are reasonable but not comprehensive. The field has moved toward neural sequence and attention models; a comparison to a simple recurrent/neural classifier that consumes the sequence of evidence sentences (e.g., BiLSTM over sentence embeddings) would help establish whether the gains come from structure or from hand-crafted features.
- More details on annotation quality and protocol are needed. CrowdFlower confidence is reported (0.92) but standard inter-annotator agreement metrics (Cohen’s kappa / Krippendorff’s alpha) and examples of borderline/ambiguous cases would clarify label reliability.
- Model selection details are somewhat ad-hoc: choice of number of latent states H differs widely between datasets (2 vs 15). A clearer explanation of H selection, sensitivity analysis, and whether the learned states correspond to interpretable narrative stages would be useful.
- The training algorithm is described as similar to latent structured perceptron / hard-EM; more information about convergence, number of iterations, initialization strategies, and hyperparameters would improve reproducibility.
- Error analysis is limited in the presented text. A qualitative analysis of model errors (e.g., failures due to coreference, implicit world knowledge, sarcastic/ironic cues) would provide deeper insight and guide future work.
- Reliance on preprocessing (coreference, dependency parses, connotation lexicon) introduces noise. Discuss the robustness of the approach to noisy preprocessing.

Overall recommendation: This is a solid, well-motivated contribution that introduces a new micro-reading task, datasets, and a reasonable modeling approach that explicitly models narrative flow. With expanded discussion of limitations, additional baselines (neural sequence models), and more annotation/model analysis, the paper would be further strengthened. I recommend acceptance.
